findings.description: Inverted expiry predicate (`age < self.ttl` instead of `>= self.ttl`) nullifies the entire L1 cache: every FRESH local hit is deleted and forced down to Redis (pickle.loads + network), while STALE entries are served as hits and never evicted — defeating the two-tier design and adding a constant L2 round-trip to the hot path. (Root cause is a logic bug; correctness detail deferred to critic-logic, but the performance regression is severe.)

L1 `_local` dict is unbounded — no max-size/LRU cap, and TTL is the only intended bound yet eviction is lazy (only on access) and broken; in a web service with a large/unbounded keyspace the in-process cache grows without limit, risking OOM.

`warm()` calls `set()` per entry, issuing one separate `redis.setex()` round-trip each (write-side N+1); bulk pre-population of many keys should use a Redis pipeline / MSET to avoid N network round-trips.

pickle.loads/dumps on every L2 fill and every set() is slower than json/msgpack for simple payloads (and a serialization footgun — security angle deferred to critic-security); acceptable only if values are genuinely arbitrary Python objects.

`self.stats.local_entries = len(self._local)` is a redundant write — the field is never read because export_stats() recomputes `len(self._local)` directly; cheap (O(1)) but dead/duplicated bookkeeping.
`time.time()` is called twice on the L2-fill path (once in `_is_expired`, again here); negligible cost, micro-optimization only.
